title: Ultrasound-Guided Versus Landmark-Based Subclavian Vein Catheterization: A Tertiary Care Center Experience
author: Major Dr. Bimal Ahluwalia
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
Background:  Central venous catheterization through the subclavian vein remains a fundamental procedure in critical care and perioperative management. While the landmark-based technique has been the traditional approach, ultrasound guidance has emerged as a potentially safer alternative. However, comparative data from tertiary care settings remain limited. This study aimed to compare the efficacy and safety of ultrasound-guided versus landmark-based subclavian vein catheterization. 
  Methods:  This comparative observational study was conducted at a tertiary care center from June 2024 to July 2025. A total of 200 patients requiring subclavian central venous access were enrolled and allocated into two groups: ultrasound-guided (n=100) and landmark-based (n=100) techniques. Primary outcomes included first-attempt success rate, overall success rate, and procedural complications. Secondary outcomes comprised procedure time, number of attempts, and patient satisfaction scores. Data were analyzed using appropriate statistical tests with p<0.05 considered significant. 
  Results:  The ultrasound-guided group demonstrated significantly higher first-attempt success rates compared to the landmark-based group (87% vs 64%, p<0.001). Overall success rates were 98% and 91% respectively (p=0.021). Complication rates were significantly lower in the ultrasound-guided group (8% vs 23%, p=0.003), particularly arterial puncture (2% vs 12%, p=0.006) and pneumothorax (1% vs 6%, p=0.048). Mean procedure time was shorter in the ultrasound-guided group (8.4±2.3 minutes vs 11.2±3.8 minutes, p<0.001). 
  Conclusion:  Ultrasound-guided subclavian vein catheterization demonstrated superior first-attempt success rates, reduced complications, and shorter procedure times compared to the landmark-based technique. These findings support the adoption of ultrasound guidance as the preferred method for subclavian central venous access in tertiary care settings.
